proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'black box' assumption that a single-day behavioral intervention (walking/biking) will causally trigger complex, multi-year systemic shifts (e.g., 10% recycling increase, 5% solar adoption, 2% electric bus fleet conversion). The causal chain from 'awareness' to 'legislative approval' and 'infrastructure deployment' is unsupported by empirical evidence. Furthermore, the technical feasibility of the proposed app is questionable; it assumes Google will develop a custom algorithm for a one-off event and that the government will mandate route modifications based on a single day's data, which contradicts standard urban planning cycles.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice with extensive literature (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Week). The proposal adds a smartphone tracking component, but this is a standard feature in existing fitness and mobility apps. The novelty is low; the proposal repackages existing awareness campaigns without introducing a new mechanism for behavior change or a novel technological solution.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are scientifically unsound due to a fundamental error in attribution. The proposal calculates long-term GHG savings (e.g., 70,205 tCO2e from solar, 35,700 tCO2e from electric buses) as direct results of a one-day walking event. These are infrastructure and policy outcomes that require years of capital investment and legislative action, not a single day of walking. The short-term impact (66.46 tCO2e/day) is plausible but negligible at a global scale. The extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e/year' globally is a gross overestimation based on the false premise that the long-term systemic changes will occur automatically and globally.

presentation.rationale: The presentation suffers from significant logical gaps and a lack of rigorous data handling. The text contains typos ('kilometrers', 'aligorithm'), inconsistent units, and a confusing narrative flow. Most critically, the impact section presents a 'wish list' of policy outcomes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) as if they are direct, calculable consequences of the event, failing to distinguish between correlation and causation. The reliance on 'Google' to build a custom app and the assumption of government mandate for route changes without a detailed implementation plan undermines the technical credibility.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a short-term awareness event with long-term structural transformation. While the intent to promote active transport is sound, the causal mechanisms proposed to achieve the stated long-term impacts (solar adoption, electric bus fleets) are non-existent. The proposal treats complex socio-technical systems as linear inputs where 'awareness' directly yields 'infrastructure,' ignoring the massive friction, capital requirements, and policy inertia involved. The quantitative impact model is fundamentally flawed by attributing multi-year infrastructure gains to a single-day behavioral nudge.
key_concerns: Causal Fallacy: The proposal incorrectly attributes long-term infrastructure and policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus deployment) to a single-day walking event. There is no empirical evidence that a one-day event causes legislative or capital investment shifts of this magnitude.

Attribution Error: The calculation of 'long term' savings (105,937 tCO2e/year) includes emissions reductions from solar and electric buses that would likely occur regardless of this specific event, or require years of separate planning. This inflates the impact by orders of magnitude.

Technical Feasibility: The assumption that Google will develop a custom algorithm for a one-off event and that the government will modify bus routes based on a single day's 'big data' is highly implausible and ignores standard operational protocols.

Behavioral Persistence: The proposal assumes a 'one-day' event will permanently alter habits (e.g., weekly home office, daily walking) without addressing the known 'rebound effect' or the difficulty of sustaining behavior change without continuous incentives.
Data Integrity: The proposal relies on self-reported data via an app for GHG calculations without specifying validation mechanisms, leading to high uncertainty in the reported metrics.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption of government and corporate compliance without a defined regulatory pathway. It demands the government to 'notify companies' to allow home office and 'arrange with transportation' to modify routes for a single-day event, ignoring the rigid labor laws, union contracts, and logistical inertia of public transit systems in Argentina. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily track data via a new app introduces significant technical and adoption risks that are not mitigated by the current plan.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day) with no novel governance mechanisms or policy innovations proposed. The proposal attempts to rebrand existing behaviors as a new 'project' without introducing new institutional levers, data governance frameworks, or incentive structures that differ from standard public awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically unsound and institutionally unrealistic. The proposal conflates a one-day behavioral shift with long-term structural policy outcomes (e.g., claiming a single event will lead to the approval of a 'Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law' or a 2% implementation of electric buses). The extrapolation of 39 million tCO2e/year globally based on a single-day event in Buenos Aires is a logical fallacy that ignores the difference between a temporary anomaly and sustained systemic change. The actual GHG reduction from a single day of walking is negligible on a climate scale.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structurally fragmented, mixing a one-day event with a 5-year policy roadmap without distinguishing between the two. It cites image files (e.g., 'main-objections.png') that are not present in the text, rendering the argument incomplete. The tone oscillates between a grassroots campaign and a high-level policy mandate, failing to define the specific governance role of the 'LGC Team' versus the State. The lack of a clear regulatory strategy for data privacy (tracking citizens' movements) is a critical omission for a public-facing proposal.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory feasibility standpoint, this proposal is a classic case of 'solutionism' that ignores institutional constraints. It treats complex public policy outcomes (legislative approval, transit route changes, corporate labor policy shifts) as simple outputs of a one-day awareness event. The proposal lacks a realistic theory of change regarding how voluntary participation translates into binding regulatory or structural shifts. It assumes a level of government agility and corporate cooperation that is rarely present in the real world, particularly in the context of Buenos Aires' specific political and labor landscape.
key_concerns: Regulatory Overreach: The proposal assumes the government can unilaterally mandate or coordinate 'home office' days and transit route changes for a single event without legislative or union negotiation.
Data Governance Gap: There is no mention of data privacy laws (e.g., Argentina's Personal Data Protection Law) regarding the collection of citizen movement data via a smartphone app.
Causal Fallacy: The proposal incorrectly attributes long-term structural policy wins (solar law, electric bus fleet) to a single-day awareness event, confusing correlation with causation.
Implementation Reality: The assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily download and use a new tracking app for a one-day event is highly optimistic and lacks a user acquisition strategy.
Institutional Fit: The proposal fails to identify which specific government agency has the authority to 'notify companies' or 'arrange transportation,' creating an accountability vacuum.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic bullet' assumption: that the government will mandate route modifications and that Google will build a custom tracking app for free. The operational complexity of coordinating a city-wide behavioral shift (5% of 2.89M people) without a dedicated budget or existing infrastructure is underestimated. The reliance on voluntary home-office adoption by companies without regulatory teeth or financial incentives makes the 'long-term' scaling plan highly fragile.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' day with app tracking and media coverage—is a well-worn trope in urban sustainability (e.g., 'Bike to Work Day' exists globally). The proposal adds little new methodology, merely repackaging existing 'Ecobici' data and generic footprint calculators. The 'business model' is essentially a sponsorship drive, which is standard for NGOs, not an innovative mechanism.

impact.rationale: The quantitative claims are mathematically unsound and dangerously optimistic. Extrapolating a single-day event's impact to a 0.38% reduction in global transportation emissions (39 million tCO2e/year) is a logical fallacy; a one-day behavioral change does not compound into annual structural shifts without sustained, enforced policy. The projected 10% increase in recycling or solar adoption based on a single walking event lacks any causal mechanism or pilot data.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is riddled with operational gaps and logical leaps. It references images (e.g., 'main-objections.png') that are not provided, making the argument incomplete. The financial model is non-existent (relying on 'sponsors' and 'government support' without a clear value exchange for the sponsors beyond vague 'brand prestige'). The timeline assumes immediate government authorization and app development, ignoring procurement and regulatory realities.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' masquerading as a scalable climate solution. It fails to address the hard constraints of operational feasibility: who pays for the app, who manages the crowd control, and how to convert a one-day novelty into a permanent habit. The impact calculations are speculative fiction rather than engineering estimates, and the reliance on free labor from tech giants and government mandates renders the rollout plan unrealistic.
key_concerns: The assumption that Google will develop a custom, route-tracking app for a single event without a commercial contract is a critical failure point.
The extrapolation of a one-day event to 39 million tons of annual CO2 savings is mathematically invalid and ignores the decay of behavioral change.
No clear funding mechanism is identified for the 'security forces,' 'health support,' and 'prizes' beyond vague 'sponsorships' which are unlikely to cover operational costs for a city-wide event.
The proposal assumes companies will voluntarily adopt home-office policies and governments will modify bus routes based on a single awareness day, ignoring the inertia of institutional bureaucracy.
The 'Business Model Canvas' is referenced but not detailed, leaving the value proposition for sponsors undefined beyond generic 'goodwill'.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that the government will instantly re-route public transport and force companies to allow home office for a single day without a pilot or legal framework. The requirement for a custom Google-developed app to track specific GHG savings via route mapping is technically and logistically unrealistic for a one-off event. Furthermore, the plan to have 5% of the population (approx. 145,000 people) walk to work in a city with high density but potentially poor pedestrian infrastructure ignores the reality of safety, weather, and the 'last mile' problem for those living far from work.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work Day' or 'Bike to Work Day'—is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada and the local 'Ecobici' system. The only slight variation is the heavy reliance on a specific app for data tracking, but the underlying behavioral intervention is standard and lacks a unique mechanism for engagement or retention.

impact.rationale: The proposal's impact claims are dangerously inflated and mathematically unsound. It projects long-term systemic changes (e.g., 10% increase in recycling, 5% solar coverage, 2% electric bus fleet) as direct results of a single day of walking. This is a classic 'slippery slope' fallacy; a one-day event cannot force legislative changes or infrastructure overhauls. The short-term savings (66 tCO2e) are negligible in the context of a city's annual emissions, and the extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e' globally is a statistical fantasy that ignores the lack of infrastructure in other countries.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is cluttered with broken image references (e.g., 'main-objections.png', 'Business Model Canvas v2.0.png') and disjointed logic. The text oscillates between a one-day event and a multi-year policy overhaul without clear distinction. The tone is overly optimistic and dismissive of real-world frictions (e.g., 'If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space...'), which alienates the very population it aims to serve. The reliance on 'Big Data' from a single day to solve complex urban planning issues is presented as a silver bullet.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down exercise in 'feel-good' activism that ignores the daily realities of the working class. It assumes everyone can simply 'walk' or 'bike' without considering safety, distance, or the physical limitations of the population. The promise that a single day of walking will magically trigger massive infrastructure and policy changes is not just naive; it risks creating cynicism among residents who see no immediate improvement in their streets or transport options. The focus on data collection via an app feels extractive rather than supportive of the community's actual needs.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes a 'one-size-fits-all' solution for a city with extreme socioeconomic disparities; low-income workers often live in peripheries with no safe walking paths and cannot afford to skip public transport or work from home.
The claim that a one-day event will lead to a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of solar laws is a logical non-sequitur that undermines the proposal's credibility.
Safety is completely ignored: encouraging walking and cycling in Buenos Aires without addressing traffic violence, lack of bike lanes, or pedestrian safety is dangerous for participants.

The 'Big Data' promise is hollow; a single day of data cannot inform long-term urban planning, and the proposal offers no mechanism for how this data will actually be used to improve the lives of the participants.
The proposal excludes those with disabilities or mobility issues by framing 'walking' as the primary solution, failing to address accessibility for the most vulnerable populations.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that the government will unilaterally mandate route modifications and force companies to allow home office for a single-day event without a clear political or economic incentive structure. The reliance on Google to develop a custom app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate in a one-off event without robust infrastructure or behavioral change mechanisms renders the operational feasibility highly speculative.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal adds a data-tracking layer, but the underlying mechanism is not novel. The novelty is further diluted by the proposal's own admission that it is a 'practice' rather than a new project, and the reliance on existing 'ecobici' programs.

impact.rationale: The proposal commits a severe normative and mathematical error by extrapolating a one-day event's impact to a 5-year horizon and then to the entire world population. The claim that a single day of walking will lead to a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws is a non-sequitur that ignores the complexity of systemic change. The 'impact' is inflated by assuming that awareness translates linearly to structural policy shifts (e.g., electric bus implementation) without intermediate steps or evidence of causality.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is structurally weak, mixing a one-day event with long-term policy forecasts in a way that obscures the actual scope. The text contains significant grammatical errors, inconsistent formatting, and a confusing 'Business Model Canvas' reference without the actual canvas. The data presentation is misleading, presenting speculative long-term policy outcomes as direct results of a single awareness day.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal masks a lack of structural depth behind inflated impact metrics. It treats complex systemic shifts (recycling rates, solar law approval, electric bus fleets) as automatic byproducts of a single day of walking, ignoring the political economy and distributional realities required to achieve such changes. The 'awareness' model assumes that individual moral choice can bypass the need for robust public infrastructure and policy enforcement, which is a dangerous oversimplification of climate justice.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The proposal assumes universal ability to walk or bike, ignoring those with disabilities, the elderly, or those living in areas with unsafe pedestrian infrastructure, effectively excluding the most vulnerable from the 'solution'.

False Causality: The claim that a one-day event will cause a 10% rise in recycling or the passage of specific laws is a logical fallacy that hides the actual, much harder work of policy advocacy and infrastructure investment.

Data Colonialism: The reliance on a Google-developed app to harvest 'big data' on citizen movement raises significant privacy concerns and risks commodifying citizen behavior for corporate gain under the guise of altruism.

Normative Trade-off: The proposal prioritizes 'awareness' (a soft metric) over 'enforcement' or 'infrastructure' (hard metrics), potentially letting governments off the hook for failing to provide safe, accessible public transport while blaming individuals for not walking.

Feasibility Gap: The assumption that the government will modify bus routes and companies will mandate home office for a single day without a binding agreement or economic incentive is politically naive.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a fragile chain of assumptions: that a single-day event will trigger government mandates for home office, that sponsors will fund a complex app, and that the government will re-route public transport based on one day of data. The claim that '5% of the population' will voluntarily walk to work in a dense city like Buenos Aires without structural incentives (e.g., parking bans, congestion pricing) is highly optimistic and ignores the systemic inertia of commuting habits.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' days and 'Bike to Work' initiatives is well-established globally (as the proposal itself admits by citing US/Canada examples). The novelty is limited to the specific aggregation of these existing ideas with a smartphone tracking app, which is a standard feature in modern fitness and sustainability apps, not a systemic innovation.

impact.rationale: The proposal commits a critical systems error by extrapolating a one-day behavioral anomaly into long-term structural changes (e.g., 10% recycling increase, 5% solar adoption, 2% electric bus fleet conversion). A single day of walking does not causally lead to the approval of distributed solar laws or the procurement of electric buses. The long-term impact is speculative and lacks a mechanism for habit persistence beyond the event day, failing to address the resilience of the current transport system.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts quantitative rigor. However, the presentation of the 'Long term impacts' section is misleading; it presents highly uncertain policy outcomes (solar law approval, bus electrification) as direct, calculable consequences of a walking event, obscuring the massive gap between a one-day awareness campaign and multi-year infrastructure and legislative shifts.

A6.perspective_summary: This proposal confuses a temporary behavioral nudge with a systemic transformation. While raising awareness is valuable, the projection that a single 'Walk Day' will catalyze the approval of solar laws, the electrification of bus fleets, and a 10% rise in recycling is a dangerous oversimplification of how societal change occurs. It risks creating a 'feel-good' illusion of impact without addressing the deep structural barriers (urban design, energy infrastructure, economic incentives) required for genuine long-term resilience.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day walking event and the approval of complex legislation (Distributed Solar Law) or the procurement of electric buses is non-existent; this is a category error in impact assessment.

The proposal assumes that 'awareness' alone is sufficient to shift entrenched commuting behaviors and infrastructure investment, ignoring the need for hard policy interventions (e.g., congestion pricing, parking restrictions).
The 'Big Data' argument is weak: one day of data cannot reliably predict long-term infrastructure needs or justify major route modifications for public transport.
There is no plan for habit retention; without structural changes, participants will likely revert to car use the day after the event, resulting in zero long-term resilience.
The reliance on voluntary corporate cooperation for home office days is a weak lever for systemic change compared to regulatory mandates.

conflicts.summary: A6 assigns a score of 3 to presentation, citing 'clear sections' and 'attempts quantitative rigor,' while A1 through A5 uniformly assign a score of 2, citing 'significant logical gaps,' 'typos,' 'broken image references,' and 'misleading data presentation.' A6 appears to overlook the structural fragmentation and the critical error of presenting speculative policy outcomes as direct, calculable consequences, which the other five experts identify as a fundamental failure of the proposal's narrative logic.

clarification_requests.question: You awarded a score of 3 for presentation, noting 'clear sections' and 'quantitative rigor,' while five other experts scored it 2 due to 'logical gaps,' 'typos,' and the 'misleading' conflation of a one-day event with multi-year policy outcomes. Specifically, how do you justify rating the presentation as 'rigorous' when the core impact section presents highly uncertain, long-term legislative and infrastructure outcomes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus fleets) as direct, calculable mathematical consequences of a single-day walking event? Does your assessment of 'clarity' override the substantive logical fallacy identified by the other experts, or did you interpret the proposal's claims differently regarding the causal link between the event and the long-term impacts?

clarification_responses.question: You awarded a score of 3 for presentation, noting 'clear sections' and 'quantitative rigor,' while five other experts scored it 2 due to 'logical gaps,' 'typos,' and the 'misleading' conflation of a one-day event with multi-year policy outcomes. Specifically, how do you justify rating the presentation as 'rigorous' when the core impact section presents highly uncertain, long-term legislative and infrastructure outcomes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus fleets) as direct, calculable mathematical consequences of a single-day walking event? Does your assessment of 'clarity' override the substantive logical fallacy identified by the other experts, or did you interpret the proposal's claims differently regarding the causal link between the event and the long-term impacts?
clarification_responses.answer:
The challenge from my fellow experts is valid and exposes a critical flaw in my initial assessment of the 'presentation' dimension. I must concede that my rating of 3 was an error in judgment regarding the definition of 'rigor.'

In my initial review, I conflated the *structural formatting* of the document (clear headings, presence of numbers) with *substantive logical rigor*. However, from a long-term impact and systems-thinking perspective, 'rigor' cannot exist if the underlying causal logic is fundamentally broken. Presenting a one-day behavioral nudge as the direct mathematical driver for multi-year legislative approvals (Distributed Solar Law) and infrastructure procurement (Electric Bus fleets) is not just a 'logical gap'; it is a systemic hallucination that misrepresents the mechanism of change.

By assigning a 3, I inadvertently validated a dangerous narrative: that complex, slow-moving systemic shifts (policy, infrastructure) can be modeled as linear, immediate outputs of a single event. This obscures the reality that such changes require decades of political will, capital investment, and structural reform, none of which are triggered by a 'Walk Day.' The proposal's presentation is misleading because it uses the veneer of quantitative precision (calculating tons of CO2 saved from a hypothetical 10% recycling increase) to mask a complete lack of causal mechanism. This is the opposite of rigor; it is 'greenwashing' via spreadsheet.

Therefore, I revise my score for **presentation** from 3 to 2. The presentation fails to meet the standard of clarity because it actively obscures the disconnect between the intervention (a one-day event) and the claimed outcomes (decades of policy change). A rigorous presentation would explicitly frame these long-term goals as aspirational 'vision' scenarios rather than calculable 'impact' metrics derived from the event data. The other experts were correct to identify this as a fundamental failure of communication integrity, not just a minor stylistic issue.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'black box' assumption that a single-day behavioral intervention will causally trigger complex, multi-year systemic shifts (e.g., legislative approval for solar laws, electric bus fleet conversion). The technical feasibility is critically undermined by the assumption that Google will develop a custom tracking algorithm for a one-off event and that the government will mandate route modifications based on a single day's data. The operational plan ignores the rigid labor laws, union contracts, and logistical inertia of public transit systems in Argentina, making the required government and corporate compliance highly improbable without a defined regulatory pathway.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice with extensive literature (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Week). The proposal adds a smartphone tracking component, but this is a standard feature in existing fitness and mobility apps. The novelty is low; the proposal repackages existing awareness campaigns without introducing a new mechanism for behavior change, a novel technological solution, or a unique governance framework.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are scientifically unsound due to a fundamental attribution error. The proposal calculates long-term GHG savings (e.g., 70,205 tCO2e from solar, 35,700 tCO2e from electric buses) as direct results of a one-day walking event. These are infrastructure and policy outcomes requiring years of capital investment and legislative action, not a single day of walking. The short-term impact (66.46 tCO2e/day) is plausible but negligible at a global scale. The extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e/year' globally is a gross overestimation based on the false premise that the long-term systemic changes will occur automatically and globally.

presentation.rationale: The presentation suffers from significant logical gaps and a lack of rigorous data handling. The text contains typos, inconsistent units, and a confusing narrative flow. Most critically, the impact section presents a 'wish list' of policy outcomes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) as if they are direct, calculable consequences of the event, failing to distinguish between correlation and causation. The reliance on 'Google' to build a custom app and the assumption of government mandate for route changes without a detailed implementation plan undermines the technical credibility. A consensus was reached that the structural formatting does not compensate for the substantive logical fallacies.

final.synthesis: The expert panel reached a unanimous consensus (A1-A6) that the proposal is fundamentally flawed in its theory of change, despite the shared intent to promote active transport. The primary disagreement initially arose from Expert A6, who initially scored 'Presentation' as 3, citing structural clarity. However, after a rigorous challenge regarding the 'greenwashing' nature of presenting speculative policy outcomes as calculable metrics, A6 revised their score to 2, aligning with the other experts. The synthesis resolves this by affirming that 'clarity' cannot exist when the underlying causal logic is broken; the presentation actively obscures the disconnect between a one-day event and decades of policy change. All experts agreed that the proposal conflates a short-term awareness event with long-term structural transformation, treating complex socio-technical systems as linear inputs. The final judgment reflects a low score across all dimensions because the proposal fails to provide a realistic mechanism for achieving its stated goals, relies on implausible technical and political assumptions, and presents mathematically unsound impact projections.
final.uncertainty: While the consensus on the proposal's flaws is strong, a minor uncertainty remains regarding the potential for the 'awareness' component to serve as a catalyst for future, separate policy initiatives if the event were re-scoped as a pilot for a long-term advocacy campaign rather than a standalone solution. The current proposal, however, does not articulate this distinction, leaving the 'long-term impact' claims as the primary unresolved point of contention between the proposal's aspirations and the experts' assessment of reality. Additionally, the specific data privacy implications of the proposed app in the Argentine context were noted as a gap, though not the primary driver of the low scores.
